questions.question: What is the difference between union types (`A | B`) and intersection types (`A & B`)?

choices.a: Union means the value has ALL of A and B's properties; intersection means it has EITHER A's or B's
choices.b: Union means the value is EITHER an A or a B; intersection means the value has ALL properties of BOTH A and B
choices.c: They are identical — just different syntax
choices.d: Intersection only works with primitives; union only with objects

questions.question: What are branded/opaque types and why are they used?

choices.a: Types that add a marketing brand to library types
choices.b: Nominal typing simulation using intersection with a unique tag, preventing accidental structural equivalence
choices.c: Types that extend `Brand` from a utility library
choices.d: Branded types only work with string literals

questions.question: What is a recursive type and what is its main limitation before TypeScript 4.1?

choices.a: Recursive types reference themselves and were banned in all TypeScript versions before 5
choices.b: Recursive types can reference themselves; before TS 4.1 only interface-based recursion was supported (type aliases could not be recursive in general)
choices.d: Recursive types are only allowed in generics

questions.question: What is variance and how does TypeScript handle it for object types?

choices.a: Variance determines how subtypes relate to supertypes when types are composed — TypeScript treats object properties as covariant (read) and method params as contravariant (strict mode)
choices.b: Variance only applies to generic types
choices.c: TypeScript always uses invariance for all types
choices.d: Variance is a runtime concept unrelated to TypeScript

questions.explanation:
VARIANCE describes subtyping in composed types:
• COVARIANT: `Producer<Dog>` is a subtype of `Producer<Animal>` (subtype in → subtype out)
• CONTRAVARIANT: `Consumer<Animal>` is a subtype of `Consumer<Dog>` (flip)
• INVARIANT: neither direction is safe (read AND write)

In TypeScript:
• Object PROPERTIES are essentially COVARIANT (readable positions)
• Function PARAMETERS are CONTRAVARIANT (with `strictFunctionTypes`)
• Mutable containers like `Array` are technically COVARIANT in TS (but should be invariant — this is a soundness hole)

TS 4.7 added explicit variance annotations:
```typescript
interface Producer<out T> { produce(): T; }  // out = covariant
interface Consumer<in T> { consume(t: T): void; } // in = contravariant
```

This improves type checking performance and makes variance explicit.

questions.question: What does `Extract<T, U>` do and how does it differ from `Exclude<T, U>`?

choices.a: `Extract` removes U from T; `Exclude` keeps the U parts — opposite of each other
choices.b: `Extract<T, U>` keeps only the members of T that are assignable to U; `Exclude<T, U>` removes the members of T that are assignable to U
choices.c: They only work with object types
choices.d: `Extract` works with keys; `Exclude` works with values

questions.explanation:
Both are distributive conditional types built into TypeScript:

```typescript
type Extract<T, U> = T extends U ? T : never;  // KEEP matching
type Exclude<T, U> = T extends U ? never : T;  // REMOVE matching
```

Examples with union types:
```typescript
type A = 'a' | 'b' | 'c' | 'd';

type E = Extract<A, 'a' | 'c'>; // 'a' | 'c'  — kept matching
type X = Exclude<A, 'a' | 'c'>; // 'b' | 'd'  — removed matching
```

Non-obvious use: filtering union members by structure:
```typescript
type Events = MouseEvent | KeyboardEvent | FocusEvent;
type MouseOrFocus = Extract<Events, { target: EventTarget }>;
// Keeps only events that have a `target` property
```

questions.question: What is `as` key remapping in mapped types (TypeScript 4.1+)?

choices.a: A way to cast keys to string type
choices.b: Syntax `[K in keyof T as NewKey<K>]` that lets you transform or filter property names in a mapped type
choices.d: Key remapping replaces the original keys at runtime

questions.question: What is `NoInfer<T>` in TypeScript 5.4+?

choices.a: A utility type that prevents TypeScript from inferring a generic type parameter from a specific argument position
choices.b: A type that removes all inferred properties
choices.d: It forces TypeScript to always widen the inferred type
